result.response_content_preview:
THOUGHT:
Currently, the only citation is Sculley et al. (2015) on hidden technical debt, which covers system-level deployment challenges but does not address the core algorithmic and representational failure modes of deep learning models themselves. For a workshop focused on real-world DL pitfalls, it is critical to add a foundational paper that explains *why* models fail when deployed outside their training distribution. The most important missing citation is a comprehensive work on shortcut learning/spurious correlations, as this concept directly bridges high benchmark performance with real-world brittleness. Adding this paper will allow me to: (1) define a specific theoretical concept driving failures, (2) compare empirical findings across academic vs. real-world settings, (3) support arguments about model fragility, and (4) highlight research gaps in robust and causal representation learning. I will search for the seminal survey/paper on shortcut learning in deep neural networks.
